text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
In our last two studies we looked at the Table of Showbread; its three names, how it was built, what sat on it, and how a Kohathite family guarded the secret of baking the bread for generations. Today we take one more step. We look at what every part of that table was pointing to. Paul wrote in Colossians 2:17 that the things; the food laws, the festivals, the sabbaths “these are a shadow of things to come; the reality, however, is found in Christ.” A shadow always tells you something about the object casting it. You can see the shape, the outline, the movement. But the shadow is not the thing itself. The Table of Showbread was the shadow. Today we look at the body that cast it.

Every single detail of this table was pointing forward to something.
Nothing was accidental. Nothing was decorative without meaning. When we study it carefully, we begin to see that the table of showbread, in its entirety, was a portrait of Christ drawn in wood and gold and flour and frankincense, centuries before He was born. Let us open it detail by detail.

The table was made of acacia wood; shittim wood, the hardest and most durable wood of the desert. It does not rot. It does not warp. It does not decay, even under decades of the most intense desert heat. This wood was chosen for the Ark, the altars, the carrying poles, and the table. It was the wood of the tabernacle because it was the wood that endures.

This wood speaks of the character of Jesus Christ as a human being. Hebrews 4:15 says He was tempted in every way as we are, yet without sin. The wood that does not rot in the desert is a picture of a character that did not decay under temptation, pressure, or suffering. He was the same in private as He was in public. He was the same to enemies as He was to friends. He was the same under the pressure of the cross as He was in the peace of the countryside.
No inconsistency, no hidden coarseness, no decay at the root. The wood held.

The acacia tree itself deepens this picture. It does not grow in rich, comfortable soil with plenty of water. It grows in the desert, in hardship, in difficult conditions. Christ was not born in a palace but in an unknown location in Bethlehem. He did not grow up in privilege but in the house of a tekton; a craftsman, a worker with his hands in Nazareth of Galilee, a place so ordinary that Nathanael once asked: “Can anything good come out of Nazareth?” (John 1:46). His human character was formed in the wilderness conditions of this world, yet it never decayed. The harder the conditions, the more the quality of the wood proved itself.

Over the acacia wood, beaten sheets of pure gold were laid; covering every surface, inside and outside. The Hebrew word for gold means to shimmer.
This was not a thin coat over a cheap base. It was pure gold, wrapping every part, leaving nothing uncoated, nothing less than the best.

The gold speaks of the divine character with which Christ was anointed and in which He walked. Colossians 2:9 says: “In Him the whole fullness of what belongs to God dwells bodily.” Not a partial anointing, not the presence of God in some moments and absent in others, but the fullness; completely, bodily, in Christ. The gold was not only on the outside of the table. It covered the inside as well. And this is the important detail. Many things look good on the surface while hiding something hollow or corrupt within. The table was gold through and through. There was nothing hollow in Christ. What you saw on the outside was exactly what was on the inside; the same character, the same purity, the same anointing, all the way through.

Wood clothed in gold; human character clothed in divine character. This is what the tabernacle was showing us. And this combination appears again and again throughout the tabernacle; the Ark, the altars, the poles always wood inside, gold outside. Heaven clothing earth. The divine resting upon and covering the human. This is what we see when we look at Christ.

Around the top edge of the table ran a gold border described as a crown.
Below it ran a second gold border; a double crown. These kept the bread and the vessels from sliding off during the wilderness journey. But they also declared something about the table itself. This was royal furniture. The table wore a crown. It belonged to a King.

Hebrews 2:9 says: “We see Jesus, who was made a little lower than the angels, now crowned with glory and honour because He suffered death.” The priests of the Old Testament wore garments of glory and beauty; they were clothed with it. But our High Priest is not just clothed with glory. He is crowned with it. A crown is higher than clothing. It sits on the head. It is the mark of a king who reigns. And Revelation 19:12 tells us He wears many crowns not one.
The double crown of the table was already hinting at this.

But notice what the crown did practically. It kept the bread from falling off. Nothing could slide off this table because of the crown that secured its edges. Here is a beautiful truth: the kingly rule of Christ secures His people.
Because He is crowned, none who belong to Him can fall away and be lost.
John 10:28 says: “I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand.” The crown of the table held the bread in place through the entire wilderness journey. The crown of Christ holds His people in place through the entire wilderness journey of this age.

We have now seen the table itself; the wood, the gold, and the crown. Tomorrow we will study about the bread that sat on top of it; the fine flour, the unleavened character, the twelve equal loaves, and the frankincense that rose from the table as a fragrance before God. Each one carries its own message, and each one points precisely and beautifully to Christ. We will continue tomorrow.

title: Prayer
text:
Praise be to You, Yahweh, who fills all things and whose glory cannot be contained (1 Kings 8:27). Yet You chose to dwell among Your people first in the tabernacle, then in the temple, and then in Your Son, and now within us.

You made a table in the wilderness, covered it in gold, crowned it with a double crown, and filled it with bread that never ran out. And You did this so that one day we would understand who Your Son was. We thank You that He is the reality of every shadow. We thank You that His character did not decay under pressure, that He stood firm in every wilderness condition, and that His anointing was complete; inside and out with nothing hollow and nothing false. Help us to see Him more clearly as we study the table. And help us to be carriers of what He is; a humanity that does not decay and a character that does not waver. Crown our lives with Your honour as You have crowned Your Son and keep us from falling off the table. In Christ’s name, amen.
